concept.title: Information Typing: Matching Content to Purpose
concept.shortdesc: DITA's three core topic types — concept, task, and reference — give each piece of content a clear purpose, making it easier to write, review, and maintain.

section.title: Why does information typing matter?
section.p: When you write content, every piece has a primary purpose. You are either:

ul.li: Explaining what something is or why it matters
Teaching how to do something step by step
Providing facts, specs, or options for lookup

section.p: DITA makes this purpose explicit by giving each topic type its own XML structure. This matters because:

ul.li: Writers get a template that guides what to include (prerequisites, steps, results) instead of guessing
Reviewers can check that a task has all required steps, or that a concept actually explains the "why"
Readers always know what to expect — a task topic always has steps, a reference topic always has lookup data
Tools can validate, format, and filter content because the type is machine-readable

section.title: Concept topics: Explaining ideas
section.p: Use a concept topic when you need to answer "what is..." or "why does..." questions. Concepts use prose organized into sections.
Structure: <concept> → <conbody> → <section> elements with paragraphs, lists, and tables.
section.codeblock:
What is content reuse? Content reuse means writing a piece of content once and referencing it from multiple locations.
Why does reuse matter?

When the same safety warning appears in 12 manuals, a single-source approach ensures all 12 stay in sync when the warning changes.
section.p: When to use it: Background information, definitions, explanations of architecture or design decisions, "why we do it this way" content.

section.title: Task topics: Teaching procedures
section.p: Use a task topic when you need to answer "how do I..." questions. DITA tasks enforce a consistent structure so readers always know where to find prerequisites, steps, and expected results.
section.codeblock:
Installing DITA-OT Download and verify DITA Open Toolkit.
  • Java 17 or later installed
 Download from dita-ot.org. Unzip to your preferred location. Add bin/ to your PATH.

Run dita --version to confirm installation.
section.p: When to use it: Installation guides, configuration procedures, workflows, any "do these steps" content.

section.title: Reference topics: Lookup information
section.p: Use a reference topic when readers need to look up specific facts — parameter values, CLI options, error codes, configuration settings.
Structure: <reference> → <refbody> → tables, property lists, or syntax definitions.
section.codeblock: <reference id="cli-options"> <title>CLI Options Reference</title> <shortdesc>Command-line options and flags.</shortdesc> <refbody> <simpletable> <sthead> <stentry>Option</stentry> <stentry>Description</stentry> </sthead> <strow> <stentry><codeph>--version</codeph></stentry> <stentry>Display version information</stentry> </strow> </simpletable> </refbody> </reference>
section.p: When to use it: API references, parameter tables, error code lists, glossaries, any "look this up" content.

section.title: Quick decision guide
section.p: When creating a new topic, ask yourself:

ul.li: Am I explaining what something is or why it matters? → Concept
Am I teaching someone how to do something? → Task
Am I providing facts for someone to look up? → Reference

section.p: If a single source file contains all three, split it into separate DITA topics — one for each purpose. This is how DITA achieves modularity.

section.title: Beyond authoring: How AI uses information typing
section.p: Information typing is valuable for writing and reviewing even without AI. But when your content feeds a chatbot or search system, the topic type becomes a machine-readable signal that lets the AI format answers appropriately — showing numbered steps for tasks, prose for concepts, and tables for references. This dimension is explored in depth in Information Typing for AI.
